editableNewDialogue.text: If you were to sketch a quick demo scenario for this kind of character-engine, what would you pick as the smallest interesting world?

A compact, high-signal demo is a "salon negotiation" micro-world: 6–10 NPCs, one room (plus an antechamber), and a single social objective (secure a patronage letter, prevent a duel, broker a marriage contract). The environment has a few hard rules (time slots, etiquette violations, reputation impacts) and a few measurable state variables (trust, suspicion, obligation, leverage). That’s enough to exercise memory (who said what), emotion (insult vs praise), planning (which person to approach in what order), and action-outcome prediction (what happens if you reveal a secret).
What would the action schema look like in that micro-world—something that isn’t just free-form text?

You can use a small typed action set that compiles into both simulation updates and prompt context. Example actions: (1) SPEAK(target, intent, topic, intensity), (2) ASK(target, topic, pressure), (3) OFFER(target, concession, ask), (4) REVEAL(target, secret_id, framing), (5) OBSERVE(channel: facial|crowd|document), (6) MOVE(location), (7) WAIT(duration). Each action returns structured outcomes: state deltas (trust +0.1), flags (target_offended=true), and new memories ("A promised a favor"). The LLM still writes the natural dialogue, but it’s constrained to pick an action and justify it.
How would you test whether the planner is actually doing something, rather than the LLM bluffing coherently?

Use counterfactual and ablation tests: (a) Freeze the planner and allow only reactive actions; compare success rate on goals with the same initial seeds. (b) Hide crucial information from the LLM text context but keep it in the state store; see whether the planner retrieves and uses it via OBSERVE/QUERY rather than hallucinating. (c) Randomize transition noise in the simulator and check whether the agent adapts (replans) versus repeating a script. (d) Run identical scenarios with the same action log but different surface language; if outcomes depend on text rather than actions/state, you’re not actually grounded.
Give an example of a non-user "memory" that the system might store, just to make sure we mean the same thing.

Example (about an NPC, not the player): "Countess Irina reacted with anger when Baron K. implied her family hid debts; she later softened after receiving a public compliment." That memory would be tagged with entities (Irina, Baron K.), emotion (anger→relief), and trigger (implied_debt), so future plans can avoid repeating the trigger or exploit it deliberately.

If it helps to anchor the design direction: I’m assembling the first prototype in Python, and I keep gravitating toward a discrete PAD-like (pleasure/arousal/dominance) vector rather than a big taxonomy of named emotions, because it’s easier to feed into the planner as numbers. The tricky part is making it feel believable when it decays over time.

That constraint set (Python prototype + low-dimensional affect vector) is actually a good fit for early iteration.

On the “believable decay” issue, you can treat affect as a continuous state variable with (1) baseline attraction, (2) event-driven impulses, and (3) time-based relaxation. Concretely:

- Keep a per-character baseline PAD (their temperament).
- Convert each interaction into a delta-PAD impulse (from appraisal + sentiment features).
- Apply exponential decay back toward baseline each tick/turn, plus optional hysteresis so recent spikes linger.

Because it’s numeric, you can also bind it directly into your planning/scoring layer (e.g., dominance upweights confrontational speech acts; low pleasure biases toward risk-avoidance), without inventing a huge emotion ontology at prototype stage.

Small correction: I’m not actually avoiding heroic/battle themes. I’m fine with combat if it serves the drama. I just don’t want the game to be only tactical fighting; my main interest is still verbal/social encounters and historical/social themes.

Got it—so it’s not “without battle themes,” it’s “not battle-centric.” You’re okay with combat as a tool, but you want the core loop to remain socially driven: negotiations, reputation, etiquette, institutions, relationships, and the historical/social texture. That means we should design characters, memory/state, and planning to prioritize social stakes and consequences, while allowing violence as one possible (often costly) branch rather than the default mode.
